title: 330,000 Indian Students. 13,000 Koreans. 90 Days for Chinese Journalists.
subtitle: Trump's visa caps were covered as a numbers story abroad — and as a press freedom story that barely registered at home.
body_md:
The Trump administration finalized rules on July 16 capping foreign student visas at four years and reducing journalist visa durations to 240 days for most nationalities — and to 90 days for Chinese reporters. [AFP/France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/americas/20260716-trump-tightens-us-visa-rules-foreign-students-journalists) reported the mechanics: ending the long-standing "duration of status" framework that allowed students to stay as long as their programs required, replacing it with a fixed four-year cap. The policy cleared the way for implementation as soon as September.

Across 27 articles from 24 outlets in 16 countries, the most distinctive editorial pattern was diaspora-quantification: nearly every affected country led with the number of its nationals at risk. [Republic World](https://www.republicworld.com/world-news/us-caps-student-visas-at-4-years-ending-duration-of-status-what-it-means-for-33-lakh-indian-students-2026-07-16-132535) reported the implications for 3.3 lakh (330,000) Indian students in the U.S., translating the federal regulation into an individual-scale impact story for its domestic audience. [KBS World](https://world.kbs.co.kr/service/news_view.htm?lang=e&Seq_Code=202965) reported more than 13,000 South Korean nationals directly affected. Pakistani outlets ran three separate items on Pakistani students. Each national press covered a different version of the same policy change, calibrated to its own diaspora's exposure.

The journalist visa rule generated a separate track of coverage. [The Independent](https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/donald-trump-chinese-department-of-homeland-security-washington-homeland-security-b3016520.html) reported the two-tier structure plainly: "Foreign journalist visas set at 240 days, Chinese reporters cut to 90 days" — the discriminatory architecture spelled out in the headline. [Malay Mail](https://www.malaymail.com/news/world/2026/07/17/china-warns-of-countermeasures-after-us-tightens-visa-rules-for-journalists/227994) reported China's formal warning that it could take "countermeasures" after Washington finalized the Chinese journalist restriction. [TASS English](https://tass.com/world/2161445) provided the legal framing that no other outlet pursued: citing China's objection as a claim that the 90-day cap violated 2021 U.S.-China media agreements, framing the restriction as a treaty breach rather than a unilateral policy decision.

The silence worth examining is China's own coverage. China's foreign ministry issued a formal protest demanding the U.S. immediately revoke restrictions on Chinese journalists — covered in this corpus only through TASS's relay of Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian's statement. Chinese state media did not appear in this cluster directly. Their absence from the corpus does not mean they are silent — it means their primary coverage of the restriction was in Mandarin, filed to a domestic audience, through a bilateral diplomatic register rather than an international news one.

The pairwise similarity between Japanese and Russian coverage was 0.364 — the lowest in the corpus — reflecting how differently countries with active bilateral disputes with the U.S. parsed the same rule change. Japan's press covered the student numbers; Russian coverage (via TASS) covered the legal and diplomatic architecture. Both were covering the same regulation, but they were reading it through entirely different interpretive frameworks.

[Globe and Mail](https://www.theglobeandmail.com/world/article-trump-us-visa-regulations-international-students-journalists/) framed the rules as part of Trump's broader immigration crackdown — connecting the student visa cap to the administration's wider regulatory project. Canadian coverage treated the policy as one element of a systematic approach; South Asian coverage treated it as an immediate practical crisis. European coverage, where it appeared at all, was primarily through French-language AFP — treating the journalist restrictions as a press freedom story more than an immigration story.

The effective date is September 2026. More than 330,000 Indian students, 13,000 South Korean nationals, and an uncounted number of students from Pakistan, Vietnam, and elsewhere now face a regulatory clock on their current visa status. The journalist restrictions create a two-tier accreditation system that gives Chinese reporters roughly one-third the working time in the United States that other foreign correspondents receive. Whether that differential survives legal challenge — the TASS framing of treaty violation is the most legally substantive argument in this corpus — is the question no outlet definitively answered.
